blocks.content: He shot it over two trips — one in January, when the town was grey and stripped, and one in June, when everything was green and loud.
He filmed the water tower. The park. The street where he'd grown up. His mother's kitchen, her hands around a coffee cup.
He didn't film his father. His father was not in the film.
Daisy wrote the narration in the evenings. He would send her the day's footage and she would watch it and then write.
One passage she sent him, attached to footage of his mother's kitchen:

Some people leave. Some people stay and hold the shape of the ones who didn't. They don't call it anything — they just keep the lights on, keep the pantry stocked, keep the door unlocked in case something comes back that never does. They call that love. They call it Tuesday. They call it not thinking about it too hard.
He read it three times before he could respond.
That's the one. That's the whole film in eight sentences.
Don't make me cry before nine am.
